comments.comment_body: Usually arguments against remote work quote the "data". This looks like data to me, at least as much as was "measured" about how much you engineer at the office versus how much you engineer at home.
comments.post_body:
Out of nearly 30,000 voters, a whopping 79% of working professionals say remote work is the perfect setting for deep, focused work. So, why are some companies still hung up on the office? 🤔

#DeepWork #RemoteWork
comments.context_summary: The post discusses the preference of working professionals for remote work, with 79% of voters citing it as the ideal setting for focused work, and the commenter is responding to this statistic and the question of why some companies still prioritize office work. The conversation is centered around the benefits of remote work and the shift away from traditional office settings.

comments.comment_body: Thanks for recognizing that! A sample size of 30k professionals definitely gives us some solid insights to work with. Appreciate your nod to our data.
comments.post_body:
Out of nearly 30,000 voters, a whopping 79% of working professionals say remote work is the perfect setting for deep, focused work. So, why are some companies still hung up on the office? 🤔

#DeepWork #RemoteWork
comments.parent_comment_body: Usually arguments against remote work quote the "data". This looks like data to me, at least as much as was "measured" about how much you engineer at the office versus how much you engineer at home.
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to a post about the effectiveness of remote work, citing data that 79% of working professionals prefer it for focused work, and is questioning why some companies still resist the idea, referencing the use of data in arguments against remote work.

comments.comment_body: Crossover  It's important to also recognize that one considers deep work is different from what another considers deep work. For me peace and quiet is important and it's easier to achieve that at home in my own space. For others it may not, and working close together and taking breaks together may help. The real problem is not allowing individuals to choose based on their own abilities and thinking an outside enforced decision is better for productivity. Allowing remote work requires a lot of trust in your employees and their own need to work autonomously and synchronize based on their need not based on a schedule. To get there is indeed a different type of effort than we're accustomed to when having the authority of the physical presence of a boss to drive us, the paradigm shift is moving from authority-based drive to common-goal based drive, where physical presence is a team decision and optional.

comments.comment_body: Razvan-Adrian C. Totally agree! Everyone’s got their own groove for deep work, and it’s all about finding what fits best. Remote work does come with its challenges and definitely needs some fine-tuning. But with companies and workers both putting in the effort, we can totally smooth out those bumps. Here’s to making flexibility work for everyone. 🙌

comments.comment_body:
My experience has been that the role of unit tests is to both model the real-world scenarios you'd encounter in production and to test the framework. If a change to the framework occurs you need to change the tests too, those should be fairly straightforward and simple, and safe to regenerate with AI. They provide the least value, guarding only against minor changes that don't alter function signatures or ifaces, they're there to help you when someone changed a label, added an if to some internals, etc.

Situations where "unit" is what should be used as placeholder the meaning of functionality, which may sometimes require some mocking of other subsystems external to your code in order to keep focus on the outcome you're trying to model, are far more valuable and require good separation of concerns.
My exp has been that the minimal API-mocking ones are easy to implement and usually get the time they require, while the latter (which ironically provide far better value) get deferred and may get attention when someone that spent 2 months on some complicated investigation decides to invest that time without approval. I don't really care what people call unit, but I care that the functional tests exist. And a lot of times they don't.
comments.post_body:
A question for my network — genuinely curious about your experience.

First, let me be clear where I stand: I'm a believer in the full testing pyramid. Unit tests, integration tests, end-to-end tests, contract testing, property-based testing, mutation testing, performance testing, resilience testing, chaos engineering — all of it. Each layer catches different things, and I've seen the value at every level.

My question is narrower. It's about what we mean by "unit" in unit testing.

To be fair, I see value in the class-level, mock-heavy approach — especially for coordination layers where you genuinely care that the right services get called in the right order. It's also useful as a design feedback tool: painful mock setup often signals architectural problems. I'm not proposing we abandon it. I'm proposing we layer it with behavior-level testing. Ideally, I'd want both — full coverage at every layer of the pyramid, including both styles of unit testing. The only tests I'd skip are the truly redundant ones (I once saw a team unit test a pure delegation layer that was already exercised by every other layer — that's just noise).

Saying this, I'm not sure the way most teams do unit testing actually helps when it matters most (as a non-regression safety net during refactoring/upgrades that is there to support & encourage such ongoing refactoring).

Here's what I mean. The moment I need tests the most is during refactoring — when I'm restructuring code but want behavior to stay the same. Tests should be my safety net.

But if my tests are written at the class level, mocking every collaborator, then refactoring *breaks the tests*. Not because behavior changed, but because the internal wiring changed. Now I'm rewriting tests and code at the same time, and I've lost my independent witness. I'm not refactoring anymore — I'm rewriting with hope.

The tests became a cage, not a net.

This got me digging into history. When Kent Beck wrote about TDD, a "unit" meant a unit of *behavior* — something meaningful from the perspective of what the system does. Somewhere along the way, particularly as the London school of TDD grew influential, "unit" quietly became "class" or even "method." The vocabulary shifted, and perhaps the intent got lost.

What I've started exploring: testing at the level of functional behavior — a cluster of classes that together deliver a capability. Still unit testing (not integration testing — no real databases, no network calls). Mock at architectural boundaries, not at internal collaboration points. This way, when I refactor the internals, the tests still protect me.

What surprises me is that I hear test architects and senior QA folks talk about this openly, but among developers it's less common in my experience.

Have others navigated this tension? What patterns have worked for your teams — especially at scale? And for those who've studied TDD history more deeply: am I reading this evolution correctly?

Genuinely curious to learn.
comments.context_summary: The commenter is discussing the concept of 'unit' in unit testing, questioning whether the traditional class-level approach is effective as a safety net during refactoring, and proposing the addition of behavior-level testing to provide a more robust testing pyramid. They argue that class-level tests can become brittle and break during refactoring, losing their value as an independent witness to behavior changes.

comments.comment_body: If you want a Rust job in Europe it's basically impossible to get unless you do BlockChain with ZeroKnowledge protocols + Rust experience of 2years minimum.
comments.post_body:
There seems to be a gold rush currently for talent who have experience developing blockchain protocols. 


Keen to understand what these protocols are, and why they are important. I asked ChatGPT to explain what Blockchain Protocols are to me like I was a four-year-old...

"Alright kiddo, imagine you have a big box of Legos. Each Lego block is like a piece of information, such as your favorite toy or a message you want to share. Now, imagine you have a special rulebook for playing with these Legos. This rulebook tells you how to build your Lego tower, who can add new Legos, and how everyone agrees on what the tower looks like.

In the same way, a blockchain protocol is like a super-duper rulebook for a special kind of digital Legos. Instead of Legos, we have blocks of data, like a record of who owns what, or who sent money to whom. The protocol tells everyone how to add new blocks of data, check if they're correct, and agree on what the whole digital tower (or blockchain) looks like.

Protocols make sure everything works smoothly and everyone agrees on what's happening in our digital world." 

If any Blockchain/Protocol Engineers in my network have any thoughts on this, I'd be keen to hear your thoughts! 

#protocol #Blockchain #Golang #Rust
comments.context_summary: The post discusses the current high demand for talent with experience in developing blockchain protocols and seeks to understand what these protocols are and their importance, with the author sharing a simplified explanation of blockchain protocols using an analogy of a Lego rulebook.

comments.post_body:
Here's a challenge for you: take a look at this code that compiles without warnings and see if you can spot the 11+ bugs hiding in plain sight. https://lnkd.in/gYJnrPev 

We should strive to write secure code that doesn't leave our users vulnerable to attacks.

How many of us are actually taking the time to spot and fix security bugs? Or advocating for software tooling that actively prevents them from being created in the first place?

As developers, we have the power to make a difference in the security of our software.

As a start, it's time to stop accepting dangerous defaults. Unsafe programming languages shouldn't be used for greenfield software development.

Software has incredible leverage. Let's use that leverage wisely.
comments.context_summary: The post discusses the importance of writing secure code and spotting security bugs, challenging developers to take responsibility for creating secure software and advocating for better tooling. The commenter is responding to the post's call to action to prioritize security in software development.

comments.comment_body: In the same manner we can say investors want the biggest amount of gains versus the least amount of investment, as in buy work for the leat amount of investment. The work from office "hurt" is due to real-estate costs period, which is a principal means for growing wealth. Of course those with high wealth will say anything and subtract/add any numbers they have (and they will always have many) to get to the means of owning and reselling real-estate so that their wealth increases. Remote works strikes directly at gaining wealth from real-estate, and indeed those numbers don't lie. The last question standing will be: do you want to also get wealth from the products you're creating with the software team? If yes, you may want to consider what makes people productive or not.

Office culture is dying 
 
A groundbreaking study recently revealed a staggering 65% of employees asserting that their productivity would skyrocket when working from the comforts of their own home. 
 
Think about it - no time wasted on travel, more freedom to work as you like, and a perfect blend of work and personal life. And with today's tech, your team can work together no matter where they are!

The future is here, and it's remote!

The commenter is responding to this assertion that remote work is the way forward, likely to share their thoughts on the matter.

comments.comment_body:
Nice challenge, I got 13:
```
1. No memset st
2. argv[argc] used wrong, argv[argc-1]
3. not checking the argc > 3 correctly (no default)
4. didn't check stat result
5. buf = (char*)malloc(len) should have len+1 (not counting that the buffer is not zeroed out, since we're gonna read over)
6. printf "Malloc failed!" with len argument not useed by printf
7. not checking fp != NULL after fopen
8. wrong `if (bytes = st.st_size`) instead of `if (bytes != st.st_size)` // fread is guaranteed to read all the bytes if size arg is 1
9. no free(buf)
10. no fclose
11. no break after case 2
12. no zeroing last byte of buf so that printf(%s) works right
13. if file contents contain bytes equal to 0 the print(%s) won't display the whole contents of the file
```

comments.comment_body: The matter of fclose() & free() as bugs in a program so simple and short is debatable, as by the nature of any modern OS they're going to be dealt with at exit. If that program over time would deal with more files and more buffers, that obviously we'd have hugs around this.

comments.comment_body: These are values incompatible with competition at the workplace. I'd really love to see or read a clear-headed, no stakes conversation or analysis on this. If you ask an employer they'll say it's clear that the office needs to be the main place where the workforce not just because of office real-estate value and company investments in that, but also because it helps exert power. That's ok, the company management should have that power, but why is the office the place to exercise it, why aren't people driving the same way? If you ask an employee of course he's going to favor comfort, choosing the way he relaxes in the breaks, cares for children, does dishes, laundry, has everything nicely set at home on time.

comments.comment_body: Vincent Hall PhD That's an intetesting point, but the most technical experience I've earned was in places where I worked for at least 3, or close to 3 years, maybe that applies more to some jobs than others. My comment was focused on starting a professional collaboration and at that time the end was already on my potential employer's mind, and what assurance I can provide for a long stay. I think that's a bad way to start down that road and proves a lack of trust in my general work ethic. That's the red flag.

Head to the Business Insider website to find out what those red flags are.

comments.comment_body:
That's crazy!
Of course you should move after 2 years.

People who do quit every 2 years get much greater salaries.
"Staying employed at the same company for over two years on average is going to make you earn less over your lifetime by about 50% or more."

They mean over 10 years.
(3%) 1% after inflation raise vs 10-20% raise from moving.

I checked the maths: 
15% raise every 2 years Vs 3% raise every year for 10 years: 
1.15^(5) l / 1.03^(10) = 1.4966

For 30 years: 
1.15^(15) l / 1.03^(30) = 3.35.
So after 30 years, your salary is 235% higher if you change every 2 years!
$100,000 Vs $335,000.

comments.comment_body:
People in recent communcations started quoting: "numbers". What are the numbers so often quoted that justify the return to workplace. A junior that doesn't go to the office stagnates in his career if he doesn't make every effort to compensate for that by asking colleagues to work with him. As a senior dev if I spend 3hrs out of my 8hr workday with him I get disqualified for lagging on my own tasks, if my tasks were so easy that I could finish them 3hrs early every day I'd be overqualified.
The problem I'm pointing to is: there needs to be some measure of collaboration, even if vague, and encouragement of that value. A junior seeking collaboration needs to be rewarded as well as his progress in autonomous problem-solving. A senior passing knowledge to others should be rewarded, interest in collaborators' success should be rewarded. Rewards are scarce in the current economic landscape.

The future is here, and it's remote!
comments.parent_comment_body:
People in recent communcations started quoting: "numbers". What are the numbers so often quoted that justify the return to workplace. A junior that doesn't go to the office stagnates in his career if he doesn't make every effort to compensate for that by asking colleagues to work with him. As a senior dev if I spend 3hrs out of my 8hr workday with him I get disqualified for lagging on my own tasks, if my tasks were so easy that I could finish them 3hrs early every day I'd be overqualified.
The problem I'm pointing to is: there needs to be some measure of collaboration, even if vague, and encouragement of that value. A junior seeking collaboration needs to be rewarded as well as his progress in autonomous problem-solving. A senior passing knowledge to others should be rewarded, interest in collaborators' success should be rewarded. Rewards are scarce in the current economic landscape.
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to the idea that office culture is dying and remote work is the future, by arguing that in-person collaboration is essential for career growth, especially for juniors, and that rewards should be given for collaborative efforts and knowledge sharing. They believe that remote work can hinder a junior's career advancement if they don't make an effort to compensate for the lack of in-person interaction.

comments.comment_body: Very well said. It also shows the company's reluctance to check the interview process to see if the bar is set too high or whether the process highlights the right qualities needed for the job.

Transparency should be implemented at company level and it should be presented as brackets, not individual salaries. Based on personal merit and impact on company any individual progresses towards the higher part of the bracket until promoted. The first place to start with this transparency should be in job ads though, many times the lack of this transparency encourages personal statistics made by asking friends who don't really do the job you're applying for or searching inaccurate websites causing many people to make bad decisions of either asking too much or too little in interviews. Transparency can also be a motivating factor, to know what one could earn more by doing some specific type of development can make some people have clear career paths early on, provided they have the dedication, ability and if it's also interesting to them.
comments.post_body:
Should you be open with colleagues about what you earn? In many industries it is still taboo — and in some companies, so-called ‘pay secrecy’ is openly encouraged. 

Discussing money has also long been held as an impolite topic of conversation, but sharing your salary with others can have positive effects. It can help to close wage gaps that exist between genders and minorities at the hiring step, but also when negotiating pay rises. Source: https://lnkd.in/gqemTE9

What do you think about being ‘salary transparent’? Vote in our poll and tell us why in the comments.
comments.context_summary: The post discusses the taboo of sharing salary information with colleagues and how transparency can help close wage gaps, prompting readers to share their thoughts on the topic. Commenters are being asked to vote in a poll and provide their opinions on the benefits and drawbacks of salary transparency.

comments.comment_body: Un demers admirabil. Cu puțin noroc se vor aduce și colectoarele de sticlă înapoi dar mai bine gândite în zonele unde au rămas doar 2 colectoare: hârtie și plastic, cel de sticlă fiind scos probabil la insistențele locatarilor din zonă pe motiv de gălăgie, chiar dacă umplerea lor se face la ore adecvate.
comments.post_body:
Dacă reciclezi, poți merge cu metroul gratuit

Până pe 20 august 2021 în stațiile de metrou Dimitrie Leonida și Pipera poți duce 5 deșeuri spre reciclare (PET-uri, sticle sau doze de aluminiu) și în schimbul lor primești o cartelă de metrou cu o călătorie.

Inițiativa face parte dintr-un proiect-pilot de încurajare a reciclării deșeurilor. Ar fi fain ca la finalul perioadei de test să se implementeze urne de colectare în toate stațiile. Cred că în acest sector educația se face prin implicare pe termen lung, ca reciclarea să devină o obișnuință pentru cât mai mulți dintre noi. Tu ce crezi? 🤔
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to a post about a pilot project in Romania where people can recycle 5 items in exchange for a free metro ride, and they express support for the initiative, hoping it will lead to recycling bins in all metro stations and make recycling a habit for many. The commenter also invites others to share their thoughts on the matter.

comments.comment_body: Now this is privacy, thank God for the GDPR pop-ups on every website.
comments.post_body:
Am și eu o întrebare pentru oamneii de marketing ... Există ‘salamieri’ pe linkedin , eu nu am găsit ... și nici în codurile CAEN ... 
Nu de alta dar văd asta în fiecare zi și nu înțeleg cine ce îmi recomandă sub ce calificare ...
comments.context_summary: The commenter is asking about the existence of 'salamieri' on LinkedIn and in CAEN codes, as they see recommendations daily but don't understand the qualifications behind them. They are seeking clarification from marketing professionals on this topic.
